CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Feb-26-2024 Continued To Apr-24-24 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Mar-07-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. BRIAN JOHNSON, Esq. of Seven Hills LLP (tel: 415-926-7247) for Plaintiff.
Atty. RUSSELL FRANDSEN, Esq. of The Business Legal Group (tel: 818-915-0426) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CEDAR BAY ENTERTAINMENT LLC, TITANIC TENNESSEE, LLC's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

The Court notes Counsel Johnson failed to contest the tentative ruling in a timely fashion. The Court allows oral argument. Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants' jurisdictional motion to quash service of summons is granted.
In this Proposition 65 matter, discovery showed defendants' website sold 39 allegedly lead-containing mugs into California. (2/22/24 Brf. 4:27, 5:16, 6:6-7.) Plaintiff says Thurston v. Fairchild Collectibles of Georgia, LLC (2020) 53 Cal.App.5th 1231 "appears to be the most directly on-point decision involving internet sales." (Id. at 7:14.) Like other cases, Thurston holds that "sales must be substantial" to confer jurisdiction, and in Thurston they were: "to California addresses $320,000 to $375,000 a year." (Id. at 1235, 1240.) Here, in contrast, sales into California were roughly 2% of that. Plaintiff also relies on As You Sow v. Crawford Laboratories, Inc. (1996) 50 Cal.App.4th 1859, but that case addressed use of a paint distribution network, not internet sales.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported.(302/RBU)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Oct-02-2023 Continued To Dec-06-23 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Oct-20-2023 Continued To Dec-22-23 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES: RE: Defendant's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant's motion to quash is continued to December 22, 2023, to allow for jurisdictional discovery. The supplemental opposition is due on December 8, 2023, and the supplemental reply is due on December 15, 2023. The parties are reminded that paper courtesy copies of all filings must be provided to Department 302 on the date of filing. Local Rule 2.7B. The pending motion is not moot. The motion was filed after service of the First Amended Complaint and it is directed to that pleading. (Defendant's Points and Authorities, 5:9-12.). Plaintiff has the burden to demonstrate jurisdiction. (Snowney v. Harrah's Entertainment, Inc. (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1054, 1062.) The court considers only admissible evidence. Here, plaintiff fails to show that defendant has purposefully availed itself of California benefits. " 'The purposeful availment inquiry ... focuses on the defendant's intentionality. [Citation.] This prong is only satisfied when the defendant purposefully and voluntarily directs [its] activities toward the forum so that [it] should expect, by virtue of the benefit [it] receives, to be subject to the court's jurisdiction based on' [its] contacts with the forum." (Pavlovich v. Superior Court (2002) 29 Cal.4th 262, 269.) As You Sow v. Crawford Laboratories (1996) 50 Cal.App.4th 1859 is distinguishable. The plaintiff in As You Sow sued a paint manufacturer that directly contacted distributors in California and sold and shipped its product to distributors in California for use in California, allegedly without complying with the label warning requirements of Proposition 65. (As You Sow, 50 Cal.App.4th at 1863-1864.) As You Sow did not involve an internet site and it predates Snowney and most of the case law discussing purposeful availment by virtue of a presence on the world wide web. Most cases (including Snowney) have employed the "sliding scale" analysis and when a case falls in the mid-range of that scale, most courts have focused on the extent, as well as the nature, of the inter-activity. (Snowney, 35 Cal.4th at 1064-1065 [by "touting the proximity of their hotels to California and providing driving directions from California to their hotels" and because "many of their patrons come from California," defendants had a "substantial connection" with California].) In this case, plaintiff merely shows that consumers purchased products from defendant's website. More evidence of purposeful availment is necessary to establish specific jurisdiction. "A passive Web site that does little more than make information available to those who are interested in it is not grounds for the exercise [of] personal jurisdiction." (Pavlovich, 29 Cal.4th at 274; Advanced Tactical Ordnance Systems, LLC v. Real Action Paintball, Inc. (7th Cir. 2014) 751 F.3d 796, 803 ["Having an 'interactive website' (which hardly rules out anything in 2014) should not open a defendant up to personal jurisdiction in every spot on the planet where that interactive website is accessible"]; Shisler v. Sanfer Sports Cars, Inc. (2006) 146 Cal.App.4th 1254, 1261[defendant's Web site, which only advertised automobiles for sale and included credit application, could not, alone, establish personal jurisdiction over nonresident defendant where site did not specifically target California residents].) The apparent fact that plaintiff first contacted defendant argues against purposeful availment. E.g., Jacqueline B. v. Rawls Law Group, P.C. (2021) 68 Cal.App.5th 243, 254.

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/CEK)

***After hearing, an order is signed.
